This contract has a Type A, Type B, or Type C death benefit.

If the contract has a Type A death benefit, the no-lapse death benefit equals the greater of the basic insurance amount or the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any no-lapse monthly charges multiplied by the attained age factor.

If the contract has a Type B death benefit, the no-lapse death benefit equals the greater of (1) the basic insurance amount plus the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any no-lapse monthly charges, or (2) the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any no-lapse monthly charges multiplied by the attained age factor.

If the contract has a Type C death benefit, the no-lapse death benefit is the greater of (1) the basic insurance amount plus the lesser of (a) total premiums paid minus total withdrawals or (b) the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any monthly charge plus the product of the Type C Limiting Amount and the Type C Death Benefit Factor, or (2) the no-lapse contract fund before deduction of any monthly charges multiplied by the attained age factor.
Total premiums paid for the purpose of determining the Type C no-lapse death benefit do not include any charge to reinstate the contract.

Life insurance coverage should be based on the financial needs that will continue after your death.
Examples of ongoing financial needs include supporting a family, paying for children's education, and paying off a mortgage.
Term insurance provides lower-cost coverage for a specific period of time, known as a 'term'.
Cash value insurance may be more cost-effective if you need coverage for a longer period, such as for your lifetime.
Most term policies do not build up cash values that can be used in the future.
You should consider whether you want to leave money to charity or family when deciding on life insurance coverage.
Employer-provided life insurance may not be sufficient to meet all financial obligations.
The death benefit from employer-provided life insurance is usually less than what is needed.
If you leave your employer, you may not be able to take the employer-provided life insurance coverage with you.
Renewable term policies can be continued at the end of the term, even if health has changed, but premiums increase.
Non-renewable term policies cannot be continued and require applying for a new policy.
Whole life insurance involves fixed premium payments according to a set schedule.
Universal life insurance allows flexible premium payment patterns as long as the policy remains in force.
Variable life insurance investments directly impact cash value, offering higher growth potential but also greater risk.
Non-variable life policies often have guaranteed minimums for features like interest or cash value, but less growth potential than variable policies.

If only one beneficiary in a class has the right to be paid, that person will be the only one paid.
If two or more beneficiaries in the same class have the right to be paid, they will be paid in equal shares.
If no beneficiary in any class survives the Insured, the payment will be made in one sum to the Insured's estate.
Before making a payment, we have the right to decide what proof we need of identity, age, or other facts about beneficiaries.
If beneficiaries are not designated by name and payment is made based on proof, we will not have to make the payment again.

Monthly charges from the contract fund are discontinued on the first contract anniversary on or following the Insured's 121st birthday.
After age 121, the contract may continue to operate under its provisions, including Death Benefit and Contract Fund terms.
No premium payments may be made after age 121 except those required to keep the policy in force under the grace period.
Loans, loan repayments, and withdrawals may continue to be made after age 121.
Cash value default cannot occur on or after the first contract anniversary following the Insured's 121st birthday.
Excess contract debt default may occur if contract debt equals or exceeds the cash value.
The contract may not qualify as life insurance under Federal tax law after the Insured attains age 100.
Adverse tax consequences may result if the contract is continued after the Insured reaches age 100.
A tax advisor should be consulted before deciding to continue the contract after the Insured reaches age 100.

Life insurance is a financial product designed to provide financial protection to beneficiaries upon the death of the insured.
The primary purpose of life insurance is to replace lost income and cover expenses such as funeral costs and outstanding debts.
There are two main types of life insurance: term life insurance and permanent life insurance.
Term life insurance provides coverage for a specific period, such as 10, 20, or 30 years.
Permanent life insurance provides coverage for the entire lifetime of the insured and includes a cash value component.
Premiums for life insurance are determined by factors such as age, health, lifestyle, and the amount of coverage desired.
It is important to assess your financial needs and obligations before purchasing life insurance.
Buying life insurance before you have dependents or significant financial responsibilities may not be necessary.
